## Food Delivery Platform ŌĆö Architecture Design

### Executive Summary

A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers is a classic multi-sided marketplace with real-time coordination requirements. The architecture uses an event-driven microservices approach with clearly bounded contexts around ordering, restaurant management, delivery logistics, and payments. The design prioritizes real-time responsiveness, eventual consistency between contexts, and independent scalability of the three actor experiences.

---

### Architecture Proposal

#### Components

| Component | Responsibility | Boundaries |
|---|---|---|
| **Order Service** | Manages order lifecycle from cart ŌåÆ placed ŌåÆ confirmed ŌåÆ preparing ŌåÆ ready ŌåÆ picked-up ŌåÆ delivered | Owns order state machine. Does NOT own menu data, payment processing, or driver assignment. |
| **Restaurant Service** | Restaurant onboarding, menu management, availability, operating hours, order acceptance/rejection | Owns restaurant profile and menu catalog. Does NOT own pricing rules for promotions or delivery logistics. |
| **Customer Service** | Customer profiles, addresses, preferences, order history view | Owns customer identity and preferences. Does NOT own authentication (delegated to Identity) or payment methods. |
| **Delivery Service** | Driver onboarding, real-time location tracking, route optimization, driver assignment, delivery status | Owns driver pool, location state, and assignment algorithm. Does NOT own order details or payment splits. |
| **Payment Service** | Payment processing, refunds, restaurant payouts, driver earnings, platform fees | Owns financial transactions and ledger. Does NOT own order business logic or pricing. |
| **Pricing & Promotions Service** | Menu price overrides, surge pricing, coupons, loyalty discounts, delivery fee calculation | Owns all pricing rules. Does NOT own base menu prices (restaurant-owned) or payment execution. |
| **Notification Service** | Push notifications, SMS, email across all actors (order updates, driver approaching, restaurant alerts) | Owns delivery channels and templates. Does NOT own business logic ŌĆö reacts to domain events. |
| **Search & Discovery Service** | Restaurant search, filtering, ranking, cuisine categorization, personalized recommendations | Owns search index (read model). Does NOT own source-of-truth restaurant data. |
| **Identity & Auth Service** | Authentication, authorization, role management (customer/restaurant/driver/admin) | Owns credentials and tokens. Generic subdomain ŌĆö use off-the-shelf (Auth0, Cognito). |
| **API Gateway** | Rate limiting, request routing, authentication verification, API versioning | Owns external API surface. Does NOT own business logic. |
| **Real-Time Service** | WebSocket connections, live order tracking, driver location streaming, ETA updates | Owns connection state and pub/sub fan-out. Does NOT own business logic ŌĆö projects events to clients. |

#### Key Interfaces & Contracts

| From | To | Contract | Coupling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer App ŌåÆ API Gateway | API Gateway | REST/GraphQL over HTTPS | Sync, loose (versioned API) |
| API Gateway ŌåÆ Order Service | Order Service | `POST /orders { items, restaurantId, customerId, deliveryAddress, paymentMethodId }` | Sync, loose |
| Order Service ŌåÆ Restaurant Service | Restaurant Service | `OrderPlaced` event ŌåÆ Restaurant confirms/rejects | **Async**, loose (domain event) |
| Order Service ŌåÆ Payment Service | Payment Service | `OrderConfirmed` event ŌåÆ Payment authorization | **Async**, loose |
| Order Service ŌåÆ Delivery Service | Delivery Service | `OrderReadyForPickup` event ŌåÆ Driver assignment | **Async**, loose |
| Delivery Service ŌåÆ Real-Time Service | Real-Time Service | Driver location stream (lat/lng every 5s) via internal pub/sub | Async, moderate |
| Restaurant Service ŌåÆ Search & Discovery | Search Service | `MenuUpdated` / `RestaurantStatusChanged` events ŌåÆ Index rebuild | Async, loose |
| Payment Service ŌåÆ Restaurant Service | Restaurant Service | `PayoutCompleted` event (settlement) | Async, loose |
| All Services ŌåÆ Notification Service | Notification Service | Domain events consumed ŌåÆ Notification dispatched | Async, loose |

#### Data Flow ŌĆö Happy Path Order

```
Customer browses ŌåÆ Search Service (read model)
         Ōåō
Customer places order ŌåÆ Order Service creates order (PENDING)
         Ōåō OrderPlaced event
    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
    Ōåō         Ōåō
Restaurant   Payment Service
 Service     (authorize hold)
 (confirm)
    Ōåō RestaurantConfirmed event
    Ōåō
Order Service transitions ŌåÆ PREPARING
    Ōåō OrderReadyForPickup event (from restaurant)
    Ōåō
Delivery Service assigns driver ŌåÆ DriverAssigned event
    Ōåō
Real-Time Service streams driver location to customer
    Ōåō
Driver picks up ŌåÆ PICKED_UP
    Ōåō
Driver delivers ŌåÆ DELIVERED
    Ōåō OrderDelivered event
    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
    Ōåō         Ōåō
Payment      Notification
(capture $)  (rate your experience)
```

#### Patterns Used

| Pattern | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Event-Driven Architecture** | Decouples the three-sided marketplace. Restaurants, drivers, and customers operate on different cadences. Events allow independent scaling and evolution. |
| **CQRS (Command/Query Separation)** | Search & Discovery is a read-optimized projection of restaurant data. Order history is a read model separate from order processing. Avoids read/write contention. |
| **Saga Pattern (Choreography)** | Order placement spans multiple services (order, payment, restaurant, delivery). Choreographed saga via events with compensating actions (refund if restaurant rejects, release driver if payment fails). |
| **API Gateway** | Single entry point for mobile/web clients. Handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, versioning) without polluting services. |
| **Strangler Fig (future)** | If migrating from a monolith, services can be extracted incrementally behind the gateway. |

---

### Domain Model

#### Bounded Contexts

| Context | Classification | Aggregates | Ubiquitous Language |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ordering** | **Core** | `Order`, `Cart`, `OrderItem` | "place order", "order status", "cancel", "refund request" |
| **Restaurant Management** | **Core** | `Restaurant`, `Menu`, `MenuItem`, `OperatingSchedule` | "menu item", "available", "prep time", "accept order", "mark ready" |
| **Delivery Logistics** | **Core** | `Driver`, `Delivery`, `Route`, `DeliveryZone` | "assign driver", "pickup", "dropoff", "ETA", "delivery zone" |
| **Payments & Billing** | **Supporting** | `PaymentTransaction`, `Payout`, `PlatformFee`, `Ledger` | "authorize", "capture", "refund", "payout", "commission" |
| **Pricing** | **Supporting** | `PriceRule`, `Coupon`, `SurgePricing`, `DeliveryFee` | "surge", "discount", "delivery fee", "promo code" |
| **Customer Identity** | **Generic** | `Customer`, `Address`, `Preference` | "profile", "saved address", "favorite restaurant" |
| **Identity & Auth** | **Generic** | `User`, `Credential`, `Role` | "login", "token", "permission" ŌĆö buy, don't build |
| **Search & Discovery** | **Supporting** | Read-only projections, no aggregates | "search", "filter", "rank", "nearby" |

#### Context Map

```
Restaurant Management ŌöĆŌöĆ[Published Language]ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Ordering
        (Menu data shared via events, Ordering holds a snapshot)

Ordering ŌöĆŌöĆ[Customer-Supplier]ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Delivery Logistics
        (Ordering is upstream, Delivery conforms to order schema)

Ordering ŌöĆŌöĆ[Customer-Supplier]ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Payments & Billing
        (Ordering dictates what to charge, Payments executes)

Pricing ŌöĆŌöĆ[Conformist]ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Ordering
        (Ordering calls Pricing to resolve final price at checkout)

Restaurant Management ŌöĆŌöĆ[ACL]ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Search & Discovery
        (Search builds its own read model, never uses restaurant schema directly)

Identity & Auth ŌöĆŌöĆ[Open Host Service]ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ All Contexts
        (Standard JWT tokens, all services verify independently)
```

---

### Trade-offs

| Decision | Gains | Sacrifices | Alternatives Considered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microservices over monolith** | Independent deployment, team autonomy, targeted scaling (delivery service scales separately from restaurant service) | Distributed system complexity, eventual consistency, operational overhead | Modular monolith (good starting point ŌĆö see implementation guidance) |
| **Choreographed saga over orchestrated** | No single point of failure, services are autonomous, easier to add new reactions to events | Harder to visualize full flow, debugging requires distributed tracing, compensating actions spread across services | Orchestrated saga with a dedicated Order Saga Orchestrator (simpler to reason about, but creates a coupling bottleneck) |
| **Event-driven over synchronous** | Loose coupling, resilience (if Notification Service is down, orders still work), natural audit trail | Eventual consistency (customer might see stale data briefly), event schema evolution requires discipline | REST-based service-to-service calls (simpler but creates cascading failures) |
| **Separate Pricing Service** | Pricing rules change frequently (promotions, surge); isolating them prevents order/restaurant service churn | Additional network hop at checkout | Embed pricing in Order Service (simpler but couples pricing changes to order deployments) |
| **CQRS for Search** | Search can be optimized independently (Elasticsearch), restaurant writes don't block reads | Index lag (seconds), eventual consistency in search results | Direct database queries (doesn't scale for geospatial + full-text + filtering) |

---

### Flexibility Points

| Area | Evolution Path |
|---|---|
| **Delivery assignment algorithm** | Start with nearest-driver. Evolve to ML-based optimization considering driver capacity, route efficiency, and fairness. Algorithm is encapsulated in Delivery Service ŌĆö change is local. |
| **Payment providers** | Payment Service abstracts provider behind an interface. Swap Stripe ŌåÆ Adyen without touching other services. |
| **Notification channels** | Add new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) by extending Notification Service. No upstream changes needed. |
| **Search ranking** | Search Service owns its ranking model. Can add personalization, sponsored results, or A/B test ranking algorithms independently. |
| **Pricing strategies** | New promotion types (buy-one-get-one, tiered delivery fees, subscription discounts) are isolated in Pricing Service. |
| **Multi-tenant / white-label** | API Gateway + Identity Service can support multiple brands. Core services are tenant-aware via context headers. |

#### Rigidity Points (Intentional)

| Area | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Order state machine** | Order lifecycle (PENDING ŌåÆ CONFIRMED ŌåÆ PREPARING ŌåÆ READY ŌåÆ PICKED_UP ŌåÆ DELIVERED / CANCELLED) is a critical invariant. Changes here affect every actor and every downstream service. Protect this with rigorous versioning. |
| **Payment ledger** | Financial records are append-only, immutable. This is non-negotiable for auditability and regulatory compliance. |
| **Event schemas** | Published events are contracts. Use schema registry with backward-compatible evolution (add fields, never remove). Breaking changes require versioned events. |

---

### Risks

| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Distributed system complexity on day one** | **Critical** | Start as a **modular monolith** with clear module boundaries matching the bounded contexts above. Extract to microservices only when a specific module needs independent scaling or deployment. The architecture above is the *target state*, not the starting point. |
| **Eventual consistency confuses users** | **High** | Use optimistic UI updates on the client. WebSocket pushes correct state within seconds. Design UX to communicate "confirming your order..." rather than showing stale state. |
| **Saga failure / partial state** | **High** | Every service that participates in a saga must implement compensating actions. Dead-letter queue for failed events. Automated reconciliation job that detects stuck orders. |
| **Driver location at scale** | **Medium** | Location updates are high-frequency (every 5s ├Ś thousands of drivers). Use Redis/NATS for real-time state, not the primary database. Partition by delivery zone. |
| **Event schema evolution** | **Medium** | Schema registry (e.g., AWS EventBridge Schema Registry, Confluent). Mandate backward compatibility. CI pipeline rejects breaking schema changes. |
| **Restaurant/driver onboarding quality** | **Medium** | Bad restaurant data (wrong hours, missing items) degrades the entire platform. Invest in onboarding validation and ongoing data quality checks. |

---

### Implementation Guidance

#### Recommended Approach: Start Modular Monolith, Extract Later

**Phase 1 ŌĆö Modular Monolith (MVP)**
- Single deployable with internal module boundaries matching bounded contexts
- In-process event bus (e.g., MediatR, EventEmitter, or simple pub/sub)
- Shared database with schema-per-module (no cross-module table joins!)
- This validates the domain model before investing in distributed infrastructure

**Phase 2 ŌĆö Extract High-Value Services**
- Delivery Service first (real-time requirements, different scaling profile)
- Payment Service second (security isolation, PCI compliance boundary)
- Notification Service third (easy to extract, no transactional coupling)

**Phase 3 ŌĆö Full Service Extraction**
- Replace in-process event bus with message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS)
- Each service owns its database
- API Gateway fronts all services

#### Technology Recommendations

| Concern | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Event Bus (Phase 1) | In-process pub/sub | Zero operational overhead for MVP |
| Event Bus (Phase 3) | Kafka or SQS+SNS | Durability, replay, at-least-once delivery |
| Real-time | WebSockets via Socket.IO or dedicated service (Ably, Pusher) | Driver tracking requires sub-second latency |
| Search | Elasticsearch / OpenSearch | Geospatial queries + full-text + filtering |
| Location state | Redis with geospatial commands (`GEOADD`, `GEORADIUS`) | In-memory, fast, built-in geo support |
| Auth | Auth0 / Cognito / Clerk | Generic subdomain ŌĆö don't build this |
| Database | PostgreSQL per service (Phase 3) | Reliable, supports JSON for flexibility, strong ecosystem |

#### Patterns to Follow
- Each module/service exposes a **public API** (interface) and hides internals
- Domain events are the **only** mechanism for cross-module communication
- No module reads another module's database tables directly
- Order aggregate enforces state machine transitions ŌĆö invalid transitions throw, never silently succeed

#### Pitfalls to Avoid
- **Distributed monolith**: If you extract to microservices but every request requires synchronous calls to 5 services, you've made things worse. Events are the answer.
- **Shared database without boundaries**: If modules share tables, extraction becomes nearly impossible. Enforce schema-per-module from day one.
- **Anemic domain model**: Don't put all logic in services while aggregates are just data bags. The Order aggregate should enforce its own invariants.
- **Over-engineering the assignment algorithm**: Start with "nearest available driver." Optimize later with data. Don't build ML on day one.
- **Ignoring idempotency**: Every event handler must be idempotent. Events will be delivered more than once. Design for it.

---

### Confidence Assessment

| | |
|---|---|
| **Confidence Level** | **85%** |
| **High Confidence** | Bounded context boundaries, event-driven integration, order state machine design, modular monolith starting strategy, CQRS for search |
| **Low Confidence** | Optimal saga pattern choice (choreography vs orchestration depends on team experience), specific technology choices (depend on team skills and cloud provider), surge pricing algorithm design |
| **Assumptions** | Team is < 20 engineers (modular monolith first makes sense); platform operates in a single country initially; order volume is < 100K/day at launch; restaurants manage their own menus (no centralized catalog team) |

---

This architecture gives you clean boundaries that map to real organizational concerns (restaurant ops team, driver ops team, customer experience team, payments/finance team), a clear evolutionary path from monolith to microservices, and intentional rigidity where correctness matters most (order lifecycle, financial ledger, event contracts).
